Theory View

ACID Properties in DBMS

ACID Properties are a set of rules that ensure reliable processing of database transactions. A transaction is a sequence of operations that should be treated as a single logical unit. To guarantee the integrity of data, database systems follow these ACID properties:

ACID Breakdown

  • Atomicity: Ensures that all parts of a transaction are completed; otherwise, none of them are.
  • Consistency: Ensures that transactions take the database from one valid state to another.
  • Isolation: Ensures that transactions are executed independently.
  • Durability: Ensures that once a transaction is committed, the data is permanently saved, even in case of a failure.

1. Atomicity

Atomicity means that a transaction is an indivisible unit of work. Every part of the transaction must succeed, or the entire transaction fails. If any part of the transaction fails, all changes are rolled back, leaving the database in its original state.

Example

Consider a banking transaction where you transfer $100 from Alice’s account to Bob’s account. This transaction involves two steps:

  • Deduct $100 from Alice’s balance.
  • Add $100 to Bob’s balance.

Atomicity ensures that both steps are completed; otherwise, neither is applied. If there’s an error while adding $100 to Bob’s account, the $100 deducted from Alice’s account is also rolled back.

2. Consistency

Consistency ensures that a transaction takes the database from one valid state to another. Any data written to the database must follow all defined rules, constraints, and triggers, ensuring that data integrity is maintained.

Example

In the banking transaction example, suppose Alice’s balance cannot go below $0. If Alice has only $50 in her account, attempting to transfer $100 would violate the consistency rule, as it would leave Alice’s balance negative. Consistency prevents the transaction from going through, preserving data integrity.

3. Isolation

Isolation ensures that transactions are executed independently of one another. When multiple transactions occur at the same time, isolation prevents them from interfering with each other, making it seem as though transactions are processed sequentially.

Example

Consider two transactions occurring simultaneously:

  • Transaction A is transferring $100 from Alice to Bob.
  • Transaction B is checking Alice’s balance.

Isolation ensures that Transaction B only sees Alice’s original balance, or it waits until Transaction A completes. This avoids conflicts in transactions, such as Transaction B seeing an inconsistent balance if Transaction A is only partially complete.

4. Durability

Durability ensures that once a transaction is committed, its effects are permanently saved, even if there is a system failure afterward. The database maintains committed changes by writing them to persistent storage.

Example

In the banking transaction, after successfully transferring $100 from Alice to Bob, the transaction is committed. Even if the system crashes afterward, the committed changes will persist. Durability guarantees that Alice’s new balance and Bob’s new balance are permanently saved.

ACID Compliance in SQL Databases

ACID Compliance refers to a database system's ability to enforce ACID properties, ensuring safe and reliable transactions. SQL databases are typically ACID-compliant, which is essential for applications where data integrity is critical, such as finance, healthcare, and online commerce.

Example of ACID Compliance

BEGIN TRANSACTION;

-- Deduct items from inventory
UPDATE Inventory SET Quantity = Quantity - 1 WHERE Product_ID = 101;
UPDATE Inventory SET Quantity = Quantity - 1 WHERE Product_ID = 102;

-- Update total sales
INSERT INTO Sales (Product_ID, Sale_Amount) VALUES (101, 25.99), (102, 15.99);

-- Commit transaction
COMMIT;

In this example:

  • Atomicity: Ensures that both inventory updates and sales insertion are completed, or none are.
  • Consistency: Ensures that all database rules (such as product availability) are followed.
  • Isolation: Ensures that other transactions do not interfere with this one.
  • Durability: Ensures that changes remain after the transaction is committed, even in case of a crash.

Summary Table of ACID Properties

Property Description Example Use Case
Atomicity All parts of a transaction must complete, or none do Ensures funds are only transferred if both accounts are updated
Consistency Transactions must take the database from one valid state to another Enforces balance rules in banking transactions
Isolation Transactions execute independently of each other Prevents interference in concurrent transactions
Durability Committed transactions are permanently saved Guarantees data persistence even after a system crash
